title: Autoformer: Decomposition Transformers with Auto-Correlation for Long-Term Series Forecasting [PDF]

paragraphs.text: ## **Autoformer: Decomposition Transformers with Auto-Correlation for Long-Term Series Forecasting**
**Haixu Wu, Jiehui Xu, Jianmin Wang, Mingsheng Long (** � **)** School of Software, BNRist, Tsinghua University, China `{whx20,xjh20}@mails.tsinghua.edu.cn, {jimwang,mingsheng}@tsinghua.edu.cn`

## **Abstract**

Extending the forecasting time is a critical demand for real applications, such as extreme weather early warning and long-term energy consumption planning. This paper studies the _long-term forecasting_ problem of time series. Prior Transformerbased models adopt various self-attention mechanisms to discover the long-range dependencies. However, intricate temporal patterns of the long-term future prohibit the model from finding reliable dependencies. Also, Transformers have to adopt the sparse versions of point-wise self-attentions for long series efficiency, resulting in the information utilization bottleneck. Going beyond Transformers, we design _Autoformer_ as a novel decomposition architecture with an _Auto-Correlation_ mechanism. We break with the pre-processing convention of series decomposition and renovate it as a basic inner block of deep models. This design empowers Autoformer with progressive decomposition capacities for complex time series. Further, inspired by the stochastic process theory, we design the Auto-Correlation mechanism based on the series periodicity, which conducts the dependencies discovery and representation aggregation at the sub-series level. Auto-Correlation outperforms self-attention in both efficiency and accuracy. In long-term forecasting, Autoformer yields stateof-the-art accuracy, with a 38% relative improvement on six benchmarks, covering five practical applications: energy, traffic, economics, weather and disease. Code is available at this repository: `https://github.com/thuml/Autoformer` .

## **1 Introduction**

Time series forecasting has been widely used in energy consumption, traffic and economics planning, weather and disease propagation forecasting. In these real-world applications, one pressing demand is to extend the forecast time into the far future, which is quite meaningful for the long-term planning and early warning. Thus, in this paper, we study the _long-term forecasting_ problem of time series, characterizing itself by the large length of predicted time series. Recent deep forecasting models [41, 17, 20, 28, 23, 29, 19, 35] have achieved great progress, especially the Transformer-based models. Benefiting from the self-attention mechanism, Transformers obtain great advantage in modeling long-term dependencies for sequential data, which enables more powerful big models [7, 11].

However, the forecasting task is extremely challenging under the long-term setting. First, it is unreliable to discover the temporal dependencies directly from the long-term time series because the dependencies can be obscured by entangled temporal patterns. Second, canonical Transformers with self-attention mechanisms are computationally prohibitive for long-term forecasting because of the quadratic complexity of sequence length. Previous Transformer-based forecasting models [41, 17, 20] mainly focus on improving self-attention to a _sparse_ version. While performance is significantly improved, these models still utilize the point-wise representation aggregation. Thus, in the process of efficiency improvement, they will sacrifice the information utilization because of the sparse point-wise connections, resulting in a bottleneck for long-term forecasting of time series.

35th Conference on Neural Information Processing Systems (NeurIPS 2021).

To reason about the intricate temporal patterns, we try to take the idea of decomposition, which is a standard method in time series analysis [1, 27]. It can be used to process the complex time series and extract more predictable components. However, under the forecasting context, it can only be used as the _pre-processing_ of past series because the future is unknown [15]. This common usage limits the capabilities of decomposition and overlooks the potential future interactions among decomposed components. Thus, we attempt to go beyond pre-processing usage of decomposition and propose a generic architecture to empower the deep forecasting models with immanent capacity of progressive decomposition. Further, decomposition can ravel out the entangled temporal patterns and highlight the inherent properties of time series [15]. Benefiting from this, we try to take advantage of the series periodicity to renovate the point-wise connection in self-attention. We observe that the sub-series at the same phase position among periods often present similar temporal processes. Thus, we try to construct a series-level connection based on the process similarity derived by series periodicity.

Based on the above motivations, we propose an original **Autoformer** in place of the Transformers for long-term time series forecasting. Autoformer still follows residual and encoder-decoder structure but renovates Transformer into a decomposition forecasting architecture. By embedding our proposed decomposition blocks as the inner operators, Autoformer can progressively separate the long-term trend information from predicted hidden variables. This design allows our model to alternately decompose and refine the intermediate results during the forecasting procedure. Inspired by the stochastic process theory [8, 24], Autoformer introduces an **Auto-Correlation** mechanism in place of self-attention, which discovers the sub-series similarity based on the series periodicity and aggregates similar sub-series from underlying periods. This series-wise mechanism achieves _O_ ( _L_ log _L_ ) complexity for length- _L_ series and breaks the information utilization bottleneck by expanding the point-wise representation aggregation to sub-series level. Autoformer achieves the state-of-the-art accuracy on six benchmarks. The contributions are summarized as follows:

- To tackle the intricate temporal patterns of the long-term future, we present _Autoformer_ as a decomposition architecture and design the inner decomposition block to empower the deep forecasting model with immanent progressive decomposition capacity.

- We propose an _Auto-Correlation_ mechanism with dependencies discovery and information aggregation at the series level. Our mechanism is beyond previous self-attention family and can simultaneously benefit the computation efficiency and information utilization.
- Autoformer achieves a 38% relative improvement under the long-term setting on six benchmarks, covering five real-world applications: energy, traffic, economics, weather and disease.

## **2 Related Work**

## **2.1 Models for Time Series Forecasting**

Due to the immense importance of time series forecasting, various models have been well developed. Many time series forecasting methods start from the classic tools [32, 9]. ARIMA [6, 5] tackles the forecasting problem by transforming the non-stationary process to stationary through differencing. The filtering method is also introduced for series forecasting [18, 10]. Besides, recurrent neural networks (RNNs) models are used to model the temporal dependencies for time series [36, 26, 40, 22]. DeepAR [28] combines autoregressive methods and RNNs to model the probabilistic distribution of future series. LSTNet [19] introduces convolutional neural networks (CNNs) with recurrent-skip connections to capture the short-term and long-term temporal patterns. Attention-based RNNs [39, 30, 31] introduce the temporal attention to explore the long-range dependencies for prediction. Also, many works based on temporal convolution networks (TCN) [34, 4, 3, 29] attempt to model the temporal causality with the causal convolution. These deep forecasting models mainly focus on the temporal relation modeling by recurrent connections, temporal attention or causal convolution.

Recently, Transformers [35, 38] based on the self-attention mechanism shows great power in sequential data, such as natural language processing [11, 7], audio processing [14] and even computer vision [12, 21]. However, applying self-attention to long-term time series forecasting is computationally prohibitive because of the quadratic complexity of sequence length _L_ in both memory and time. LogTrans [20] introduces the local convolution to Transformer and proposes the LogSparse attention to select time steps following the exponentially increasing intervals, which reduces the complexity to _O_ ( _L_ (log _L_ )[2] ). Reformer [17] presents the local-sensitive hashing (LSH) attention and reduces the complexity to _O_ ( _L_ log _L_ ). Informer [41] extends Transformer with KL-divergence based ProbSparse

2

attention and also achieves _O_ ( _L_ log _L_ ) complexity. Note that these methods are based on the vanilla Transformer and try to improve the self-attention mechanism to a _sparse_ version, which still follows the point-wise dependency and aggregation. In this paper, our proposed Auto-Correlation mechanism is based on the inherent periodicity of time series and can provide series-wise connections.

## **2.2 Decomposition of Time Series**

As a standard method in time series analysis, time series decomposition [1, 27] deconstructs a time series into several components, each representing one of the underlying categories of patterns that are more predictable. It is primarily useful for exploring historical changes over time. For the forecasting tasks, decomposition is always used as the _pre-processing_ of historical series before predicting future series [15, 2], such as Prophet [33] with trend-seasonality decomposition and N-BEATS [23] with basis expansion and DeepGLO [29] with matrix decomposition. However, such pre-processing is limited by the plain decomposition effect of historical series and overlooks the hierarchical interaction between the underlying patterns of series in the long-term future. This paper takes the decomposition idea from a new progressive dimension. Our Autoformer harnesses the decomposition as an inner block of deep models, which can progressively decompose the hidden series throughout the whole forecasting process, including both the past series and the predicted intermediate results.

## **3 Autoformer**

The time series forecasting problem is to predict the most probable length- _O_ series in the future given the past length- _I_ series, denoting as _input-I-predict-O_ . The _long-term forecasting_ setting is to predict the long-term future, i.e. larger _O_ . As aforementioned, we have highlighted the difficulties of long-term series forecasting: handling intricate temporal patterns and breaking the bottleneck of computation efficiency and information utilization. To tackle these two challenges, we introduce the decomposition as a builtin block to the deep forecasting model and propose _Autoformer_ as a decomposition architecture. Besides, we design the _Auto-Correlation_ mechanism to discover the period-based dependencies and aggregate similar sub-series from underlying periods.

## **3.1 Decomposition Architecture**

We renovate Transformer [35] to a deep decomposition architecture (Figure 1), including the inner series decomposition block, Auto-Correlation mechanism, and corresponding Encoder and Decoder.

**Series decomposition block** To learn with the complex temporal patterns in long-term forecasting context, we take the idea of decomposition [1, 27], which can separate the series into trend-cyclical and seasonal parts. These two parts reflect the long-term progression and the seasonality of the series respectively. However, directly decomposing is unrealizable for future series because the future is just unknown. To tackle this dilemma, we present a _series decomposition block_ as an inner operation of Autoformer (Figure 1), which can extract the long-term stationary trend from predicted intermediate hidden variables progressively. ## **3.2 Auto-Correlation Mechanism**

As shown in Figure 2, we propose the Auto-Correlation mechanism with series-wise connections to expand the information utilization. Auto-Correlation discovers the period-based dependencies by calculating the series autocorrelation and aggregates similar sub-series by time delay aggregation.

**Period-based dependencies** It is observed that the same phase position among periods naturally provides similar sub-processes. Inspired by the stochastic process theory [8, 24], for a real discretetime process _{Xt}_ , we can obtain the autocorrelation _RXX_ ( _τ_ ) by the following equations:

_RXX_ ( _τ_ ) reflects the time-delay similarity between _{Xt}_ and its _τ_ lag series _{Xt−τ }_ . As shown in Figure 2, we use the autocorrelation _R_ ( _τ_ ) as the unnormalized confidence of estimated period length _τ_ . Then, we choose the most possible _k_ period lengths _τ_ 1 _, · · · , τk_ . The period-based dependencies are derived by the above estimated periods and can be weighted by the corresponding autocorrelation.

**Time delay aggregation** The period-based dependencies connect the sub-series among estimated periods. Thus, we present the _time delay aggregation_ block (Figure 2), which can roll the series based on selected time delay _τ_ 1 _, · · · , τk_ . This operation can align similar sub-series that are at the same phase position of estimated periods, which is different from the point-wise dot-product aggregation in self-attention family. Finally, we aggregate the sub-series by softmax normalized confidences.

**Efficient computation** For period-based dependencies, these dependencies point to sub-processes at the same phase position of underlying periods and are inherently sparse. Here, we select the most possible delays to avoid picking the opposite phases. Because we aggregate _O_ (log _L_ ) series whose length is _L_ , the complexity of Equations 6 and 7 is _O_ ( _L_ log _L_ ). For the autocorrelation computation (Equation 5), given time series _{Xt}_ , _RXX_ ( _τ_ ) can be calculated by Fast Fourier Transforms (FFT) based on the Wiener–Khinchin theorem [37]:

where _τ ∈{_ 1 _, · · · , L}_ , _F_ denotes the FFT and _F[−]_[1] is its inverse. _∗_ denotes the conjugate operation and _SXX_ ( _f_ ) is in the frequency domain. Note that the series autocorrelation of all lags in _{_ 1 _, · · · , L}_ can be calculated at once by FFT. Thus, Auto-Correlation achieves the _O_ ( _L_ log _L_ ) complexity.

**Auto-Correlation vs. self-attention family** Different from the point-wise self-attention family, Auto-Correlation presents the series-wise connections (Figure 3). Concretely, for the temporal dependencies, we find the dependencies among sub-series based on the periodicity. In contrast, the self-attention family only calculates the relation between scattered points. Though some selfattentions [20, 41] consider the local information, they only utilize this to help point-wise dependencies discovery. For the information aggregation, we adopt the time delay block to aggregate the similar sub-series from underlying periods. In contrast, self-attentions aggregate the selected points by dot-product. Benefiting from the inherent sparsity and sub-series-level representation aggregation, Auto-Correlation can simultaneously benefit the computation efficiency and information utilization.

## **4 Experiments**

We extensively evaluate the proposed Autoformer on six real-world benchmarks, covering five mainstream time series forecasting applications: energy, traffic, economics, weather and disease.

**Datasets** Here is a description of the six experiment datasets: (1) _ETT_ [41] dataset contains the data collected from electricity transformers, including load and oil temperature that are recorded every

15 minutes between July 2016 and July 2018. (2) _Electricity_[1] dataset contains the hourly electricity consumption of 321 customers from 2012 to 2014. (3) _Exchange_ [19] records the daily exchange rates of eight different countries ranging from 1990 to 2016. (4) _Traffic_[2] is a collection of hourly data from California Department of Transportation, which describes the road occupancy rates measured by different sensors on San Francisco Bay area freeways. (5) _Weather_[3] is recorded every 10 minutes for 2020 whole year, which contains 21 meteorological indicators, such as air temperature, humidity, etc. (6) _ILI_[4] includes the weekly recorded influenza-like illness (ILI) patients data from Centers for Disease Control and Prevention of the United States between 2002 and 2021, which describes the ratio of patients seen with ILI and the total number of the patients. We follow standard protocol and split all datasets into training, validation and test set in chronological order by the ratio of 6:2:2 for the ETT dataset and 7:1:2 for the other datasets.

**Implementation details** Our method is trained with L2 loss, using the ADAM [16] optimizer with an initial learning rate of 10 _[−]_[4] . Batch size is set to 32. The training process is early stopped within 10 epochs. All experiments are repeated three times, implemented in PyTorch [25] and conducted on a single NVIDIA TITAN RTX 24GB GPUs. The hyper-parameter _c_ of Auto-Correlation is in the range of 1 to 3 to trade off performance and efficiency. See supplementary materials for standard deviations and sensitivity analysis. Autoformer contains 2 encoder layers and 1 decoder layer.

**Baselines** We include 10 baseline methods. For the _multivariate_ setting, we select three latest stateof-the-art transformer-based models: Informer [41], Reformer [17], LogTrans [20], two RNN-based models: LSTNet [19], LSTM [13] and CNN-based TCN [3] as baselines. For the _univariate_ setting, we include more competitive baselines: N-BEATS[23], DeepAR [28], Prophet [33] and ARMIA [1].

## **4.1 Main Results**

To compare performances under different future horizons, we fix the input length and evaluate models with a wide range of prediction lengths: 96, 192, 336, 720. This setting precisely meets the definition of long-term forecasting. Here are results on both the multivariate and univariate settings.

**Multivariate results** As for the multivariate setting, Autoformer achieves the consistent state-ofthe-art performance in all benchmarks and all prediction length settings (Table 1). Especially, under the input-96-predict-336 setting, compared to previous state-of-the-art results, Autoformer gives **74%** (1.334 _→_ 0.339) MSE reduction in ETT, **18%** (0.280 _→_ 0.231) in Electricity, **61%** (1.357 _→_ 0.509) in Exchange, **15%** (0.733 _→_ 0.622) in Traffic and **21%** (0.455 _→_ 0.359) in Weather. For the input36-predict-60 setting of ILI, Autoformer makes **43%** (4.882 _→_ 2.770) MSE reduction. Overall, Autoformer yields a **38%** averaged MSE reduction among above settings. Note that Autoformer still provides remarkable improvements in the _Exchange_ dataset that is **without obvious periodicity** . See supplementary materials for detailed showcases. Besides, we can also find that the performance of Autoformer changes quite steadily as the prediction length _O_ increases. It means that Autoformer retains better **long-term robustness** , which is meaningful for real-world practical applications, such as weather early warning and long-term energy consumption planning.

**Univariate results** We list the univariate results of two typical datasets in Table 2. Under the comparison with extensive baselines, our Autoformer still achieves state-of-the-art performance for the long-term forecasting tasks. In particular, for the input-96-predict-336 setting, our model achieves **14%** (0.180 _→_ 0.145) MSE reduction on the ETT dataset with obvious periodicity. For the Exchange dataset without obvious periodicity, Autoformer surpasses other baselines by **17%** (0.611 _→_ 0.508) and shows greater long-term forecasting capacity. Also, we find that ARIMA [1] performs best in the input-96-predict-96 setting of the Exchange dataset but fails in the long-term setting. This situation of ARIMA can be benefited from its inherent capacity for non-stationary economic data but is limited by the intricate temporal patterns of real-world series.

## **4.3 Model Analysis**

**Time series decomposition** As shown in Figure 4, without our series decomposition block, the forecasting model cannot capture the increasing trend and peaks of the seasonal part. By adding the series decomposition blocks, Autoformer can aggregate and refine the trend-cyclical part from series progressively. This design also facilitates the learning of the seasonal part, especially the peaks and troughs. This verifies the necessity of our proposed progressive decomposition architecture.

## **5 Conclusions**

This paper studies the long-term forecasting problem of time series, which is a pressing demand for real-world applications. However, the intricate temporal patterns prevent the model from learning reliable dependencies. We propose the Autoformer as a decomposition architecture by embedding the series decomposition block as an inner operator, which can progressively aggregate the longterm trend part from intermediate prediction. Besides, we design an efficient Auto-Correlation mechanism to conduct dependencies discovery and information aggregation at the series level, which contrasts clearly from the previous self-attention family. Autoformer can naturally achieve _O_ ( _L_ log _L_ ) complexity and yield consistent state-of-the-art performance in extensive real-world datasets.

progressive__gpt-5.5.label: Progressive (gpt-5.5)
progressive__gpt-5.5.overall_feedback: The paper addresses an important and practically relevant problem—long-horizon time-series forecasting—and the proposed combination of progressive decomposition with an Auto-Correlation mechanism is well motivated at a high level and potentially impactful. The introduction is generally clear in positioning the work against Transformer-based forecasting models, and the claimed empirical gains across multiple domains suggest strong practical relevance. However, the presentation also relies heavily on broad claims that need sharper technical grounding: terms such as “information utilization bottleneck,” “series-level connection,” and “stochastic process theory” are invoked but not yet defined precisely enough to make the mechanism’s necessity or novelty fully convincing. The paper should better clarify the assumptions under which periodic sub-series similarity is expected to hold, how this differs from classical autocorrelation/FFT-based methods and prior decomposition models, and whether the architecture remains robust for weakly periodic, nonstationary, or regime-shifting series. The stated 38% improvement is compelling but potentially overbroad without careful explanation of metrics, baselines, horizons, statistical variability, and ablations isolating decomposition versus Auto-Correlation. Finally, while the writing is mostly readable, it contains some awkward phrasing, formatting artifacts, and repeated promotional language; a more precise and restrained exposition would improve credibility. Overall, the paper appears promising and ambitious, but its most significant issues are the need for stronger theoretical/technical justification, clearer novelty relative to existing time-series methods, and rigorous empirical substantiation of its large performance claims.

comments.title: Overgeneralized claim that decomposition is only/always preprocessing in forecasting
comments.quote: However, under the forecasting context, it can only be used as the _pre-processing_ of past series because the future is unknown [15]. / For the forecasting tasks, decomposition is always used as the _pre-processing_ of historical series before predicting future series [15, 2], such as Prophet [33] with trend-seasonality decomposition and N-BEATS [23] with basis expansion and DeepGLO [29] with matrix decomposition.
comments.explanation: The authors’ intended contrast is understandable: Autoformer embeds decomposition progressively inside the forecasting network, whereas many practical pipelines decompose observed history before forecasting. However, the wording “can only” and “always” is too categorical and risks mischaracterizing prior work. Forecasting models such as structural time-series/state-space models, ETS-style methods, Prophet-like additive models, N-BEATS variants, and factorization-based approaches can explicitly model, extrapolate, or jointly estimate trend and seasonal components rather than merely using decomposition as preprocessing. The novelty of inner progressive decomposition may still hold, but the claim should be qualified as “often” or “commonly” rather than presented as a universal limitation.

comments.title: Claims about self-attention and sparse attention overstate the generality of Auto-Correlation’s advantage
comments.quote: Auto-Correlation outperforms self-attention in both efficiency and accuracy. / Also, Transformers have to adopt the sparse versions of point-wise self-attentions for long series efficiency, resulting in the information utilization bottleneck.
comments.explanation: The motivation is reasonable: vanilla self-attention has quadratic cost, sparse long-sequence Transformers can lose information, and Auto-Correlation may be more efficient and accurate on the paper’s long-term forecasting benchmarks. Still, the wording is broader than the evidence can support. Efficiency depends on sequence length, dimensionality, implementation constants, FFT overhead, hardware, and which attention variant is used; accuracy is necessarily dataset- and task-dependent. Long-sequence efficiency can also be addressed by low-rank, kernelized, recurrent, compressed-memory, patching/downsampling, convolutional, or state-space approaches, not only sparse attention. The claims would be more precise if limited to the reported setting and baselines.

comments.title: Reported relative-improvement percentages are ambiguous or inconsistent with the displayed numbers
comments.quote: Autoformer yields stateof-the-art accuracy, with a 38% relative improvement on six benchmarks. / for the input-96-predict-336 setting, our model achieves 14% (0.180 → 0.145) MSE reduction on the ETT dataset. / Autoformer gives 74% (1.334 → 0.339) MSE reduction in ETT, 18% (0.280 → 0.231) in Electricity, 61% (1.357 → 0.509) in Exchange, 15% (0.733 → 0.622) in Traffic and 21% (0.455 → 0.359) in Weather. ... Overall, Autoformer yields a 38% averaged MSE reduction among above settings.
comments.explanation: The qualitative conclusion that Autoformer improves over baselines is supported, but several headline percentages are not fully reproducible from the printed values or are underspecified. For example, (0.180−0.145)/0.180 is about 19%, not 14%, and (1.357−0.509)/1.357 is about 62.5%, not 61%. The abstract’s 38% relative improvement also does not state the metric, baseline, horizons, aggregation rule, or denominator. The later “38% averaged MSE reduction among above settings” is ambiguous because averaging the listed displayed reductions gives a different value from pooled reduction, and it is unclear whether the figure instead refers to all table settings. The issue is numerical clarity and reproducibility, not necessarily the direction of the result.

comments.title: Moving-average decomposition is not fully specified
comments.quote: Concretely, we adapt the moving average to smooth out periodic fluctuations and highlight the long-term trends. ... We adopt the AvgPool(·) for moving average with the padding operation to keep the series length unchanged.
comments.explanation: The intended operation is conceptually clear: smooth the sequence to obtain a trend-cyclical component and take the residual as the seasonal component. However, the actual moving-average operator is not fully defined in the passage. AvgPool requires a window/kernel size, stride, centering convention, and boundary padding rule. Zero, replication, reflection, or circular padding can produce different endpoint behavior, and the window size determines which frequencies are assigned to trend versus residual. Without these details, the decomposition block is not fully reproducible from the prose alone.

comments.title: “Stationary trend” is technically ambiguous
comments.quote: To tackle this dilemma, we present a _series decomposition block_ as an inner operation of Autoformer (Figure 1), which can extract the long-term stationary trend from predicted intermediate hidden variables progressively.
comments.explanation: The intended meaning is likely a smooth or stable long-term trend extracted by moving average. But in standard time-series terminology, a trend is often what makes a process non-stationary, while stationarity refers to distributional invariance over time after detrending or differencing. The phrase “stationary trend” could therefore confuse readers about whether a formal stationarity assumption is being made. A term such as “smooth long-term trend” would be clearer.

comments.title: Finite-sequence FFT autocorrelation is ambiguous about circular versus linear lags
comments.quote: Roll( _X , τ_ ) represents the operation to _X_ with time delay _τ_ , during which elements that are shifted beyond the first position are re-introduced at the last position.
comments.explanation: The circular roll operation is plausible given the periodic motivation and FFT implementation. However, standard finite-sample time-series autocorrelation often refers to linear/aperiodic lags with truncation or padding, not wraparound. Computing all lags by FFT via Wiener–Khinchin is directly circular unless the sequence is zero-padded appropriately to recover linear autocorrelation. Since circular and linear lag definitions yield different boundary behavior and delay scores, the paper should explicitly state that it uses circular autocorrelation/aggregation if that is the intended mechanism.

comments.title: Top-k delay selection is underspecified for multichannel and multi-head correlations
comments.quote: where arg Topk(·) is to get the arguments of the Topk autocorrelations and let k = floor(c × log L), c is a hyper-parameter. R_Q,K is autocorrelation between series Q and K.
comments.explanation: The high-level idea is clear: compute lag correlations, choose the top-k delays, and aggregate rolled values. What remains unclear is how the top-k scores are reduced when Q and K are multivariate, batched, and multi-head tensors. R_Q,K(τ) could be a scalar per lag, a vector per channel, or head-/sample-specific. These alternatives imply materially different algorithms: a global delay set, per-head delay sets, per-sample delay sets, or per-channel delay sets. Because selected lags directly determine the sub-series aggregation and affect complexity and predictions, this reduction step should be specified for reproducibility.

comments.title: The method calls R_Q,K an autocorrelation even when it is mathematically a cross-correlation
comments.quote: R_Q,K is autocorrelation between series Q and K.
comments.explanation: The name Auto-Correlation is understandable because Q, K, and V are projections within a self-style sequence mechanism. Mathematically, however, once Q and K are distinct projected series, R_Q,K is conventionally a cross-correlation rather than an autocorrelation. The distinction is especially relevant in encoder-decoder use, where Q comes from the decoder and K,V from the encoder. The computational idea can still be valid, but the terminology should be clarified to avoid confusion about the statistic being computed.

comments.title: Overgeneralized claim that decomposition can only be preprocessing because the future is unknown
comments.quote: However, under the forecasting context, it can only be used as the _pre-processing_ of past series because the future is unknown [15].
comments.explanation: I understand the authors’ intended contrast: many practical pipelines first decompose the observed historical series and then forecast the decomposed components, whereas Autoformer embeds decomposition inside the neural architecture and applies it progressively to hidden states. That is a meaningful distinction, and the later related-work paragraph similarly frames decomposition as commonly used before forecasting. However, the wording “can only be used” is stronger than what standard forecasting practice supports. Classical and modern decomposable models often represent future trend/seasonal components explicitly, estimate them jointly, or forecast decomposed latent components rather than merely preprocessing past observations; examples include structural time-series/state-space formulations, ETS-style models, Prophet-like additive decompositions, and basis-expansion approaches. The future observations are unknown, but that does not imply decomposition is restricted to historical preprocessing. A safer statement would be that conventional decomposition is often applied as preprocessing to the observed history, which limits interaction with later learned representations.

comments.title: Claim that Auto-Correlation outperforms self-attention is stated more broadly than the evidence can generally support
comments.quote: Auto-Correlation outperforms self-attention in both efficiency and accuracy.
comments.explanation: The paper’s motivation for Auto-Correlation is clear: replacing point-wise attention with period-based sub-series aggregation plausibly reduces asymptotic cost and may improve forecasting on periodic long-horizon benchmarks. The full context also later describes an \(O(L\log L)\) computation and experiments against Transformer-family baselines, so the claim is not unsupported in spirit. What remains problematic is the unqualified breadth of “outperforms self-attention in both efficiency and accuracy.” Efficiency depends on implementation constants, sequence length, dimensionality, FFT overhead, and whether the comparator is full or sparse attention. Accuracy is empirical and dataset/task dependent; Auto-Correlation may outperform on the reported long-term time-series benchmarks, but the sentence reads as a general dominance claim over self-attention mechanisms. The wording would be more precise if limited to the evaluated long-term forecasting setting or to the reported benchmarks.

comments.title: The stated 38% relative improvement lacks enough qualification in the overview
comments.quote: In long-term forecasting, Autoformer yields stateof-the-art accuracy, with a 38% relative improvement on six benchmarks, covering five practical applications: energy, traffic, economics, weather and disease.
comments.explanation: I take this as an abstract-level summary of the experimental results, and it is common for abstracts to compress the metric, baseline, and aggregation procedure. The surrounding text later says six benchmarks are evaluated, so the high-level intent is understandable. Still, “38% relative improvement on six benchmarks” is ambiguous in a way that could affect interpretation: it does not say whether this is averaged over MSE, MAE, both, all horizons, all datasets, only multivariate settings, or relative to which baseline. Relative improvements in forecasting can differ substantially depending on whether they are computed per metric then averaged, pooled across tables, or measured against the best prior model. Since this number is a central headline result, a careful reader could reasonably wonder what exact denominator and aggregation procedure produce 38%. The issue is not that the claim is necessarily false, but that the summary is underspecified enough to be potentially misleading.

comments.title: “Have to adopt sparse versions” overstates the necessity of sparse attention for long-series efficiency
comments.quote: Also, Transformers have to adopt the sparse versions of point-wise self-attentions for long series efficiency, resulting in the information utilization bottleneck.
comments.explanation: The authors are correctly pointing to a real computational issue: vanilla self-attention has quadratic time and memory in sequence length, and many long-sequence forecasting Transformers reduce cost with sparse attention. In the immediate context, they are contrasting their Auto-Correlation mechanism against LogTrans, Reformer, and Informer-style sparsification, so the motivation is clear. However, “have to adopt” is too categorical. Long-sequence Transformer efficiency can also be addressed by low-rank/kernelized attention, recurrence/memory compression, patching/downsampling, convolutional hybrids, state-space models, or hardware/implementation choices, not only sparse point-wise attention. Even within the Transformer family, sparsity is one design route rather than a necessity. This is a framing overstatement rather than a methodological flaw, but it slightly narrows the landscape in a way that supports the proposed contrast more strongly than warranted.

comments.title: Prior decomposition methods are overgeneralized as preprocessing
comments.quote: For the forecasting tasks, decomposition is always used as the _pre-processing_ of historical series before predicting future series [15, 2], such as Prophet [33] with trend-seasonality decomposition and N-BEATS [23] with basis expansion and DeepGLO [29] with matrix decomposition.
comments.explanation: I understand the authors' intended contrast: Autoformer embeds decomposition repeatedly inside the forecasting network, whereas many classical workflows decompose the observed history before fitting or forecasting. That is a reasonable high-level motivation. However, the word "always" and the examples make the claim stronger than the context supports. Prophet is not merely a preprocessing decomposition of the historical series; it specifies an additive forecasting model with trend and seasonal components that are extrapolated into the future. N-BEATS, especially its interpretable variant, also uses basis expansions and stack structure inside the model rather than a simple preprocessing step. DeepGLO likewise combines factorization/global modeling with forecasting rather than being just historical preprocessing. The novelty of progressive inner decomposition may still hold, but this sentence risks mischaracterizing relevant prior work and overstating the contrast.

comments.title: Moving-average decomposition is not fully specified
comments.quote:
Concretely, we adapt the moving average to smooth out periodic fluctuations and highlight the long-term trends. For length- _L_ input series _X ∈_ R _[L][×][d]_ , the process is:

where _X_ s _, X_ t _∈_ R _[L][×][d]_ denote the seasonal and the extracted trend-cyclical part respectively. We adopt the AvgPool( _·_ ) for moving average with the padding operation to keep the series length unchanged.
comments.explanation: The intended operation is clear at a conceptual level: compute a smoothed trend component by average pooling and take the residual as the seasonal component. That is a sensible differentiable analogue of a moving-average decomposition. What remains underspecified is the actual moving-average operator. AvgPool requires at least a window/kernel size, stride, centering convention, and boundary padding rule. The text says padding is used to keep the length unchanged, but zero padding, replication padding, reflection padding, or circular padding produce different endpoint trends; in time-series forecasting these boundary choices can matter. The window size is especially important because it determines which frequencies are treated as trend versus seasonal residual. If these choices are specified elsewhere or in code, the method can be reproduced, but from this passage the core decomposition block is not fully defined.

comments.title: “Stationary trend” is technically ambiguous
comments.quote: To tackle this dilemma, we present a _series decomposition block_ as an inner operation of Autoformer (Figure 1), which can extract the long-term stationary trend from predicted intermediate hidden variables progressively.
comments.explanation: The surrounding text makes the authors' intent mostly recoverable: they use a moving average to extract a slowly varying trend-cyclical component from hidden variables. However, the phrase "stationary trend" is awkward in the standard time-series sense. A trend is usually the component whose presence makes a process non-stationary, while stationarity refers to distributional invariance over time after detrending or differencing. Perhaps the intended meaning is "stable," "smooth," or "long-term" trend rather than stationarity in the stochastic-process sense. Because the paper later invokes stochastic-process terminology for Auto-Correlation, a careful reader could wonder whether a formal stationarity assumption is being made for the trend component. The issue is localized, but the terminology could mislead.

comments.title: Finite-sequence FFT autocorrelation is ambiguous about circular versus linear lags
comments.quote: Roll( _X , τ_ ) represents the operation to _X_ with time delay _τ_ , during which elements that are shifted beyond the first position are re-introduced at the last position.
comments.explanation: I initially read this as a deliberate periodic/circular shift, which is natural given the authors' motivation around periodicity and their use of FFTs. That makes the operation internally plausible: if one assumes a periodic extension of the sequence, wrapping shifted elements to the end is exactly what one would do. However, the preceding stochastic-process discussion describes similarity between \({X_t}\) and its lagged series \({X_{t-\tau}}\), which in standard finite-sample time-series usage usually means an aperiodic lag with boundary truncation or padding, not a circular wraparound. Similarly, the later claim that all lags can be computed by FFT via Wiener--Khinchin is only directly true for circular autocorrelation unless the sequence is zero-padded sufficiently to recover the linear autocorrelation. The paper may indeed intend circular autocorrelation because the subsequent aggregation uses circular Roll, but this choice should be stated explicitly. Otherwise a reader could implement the standard linear autocorrelation and obtain different delay scores and boundary behavior.

comments.title: Top-k delay selection is underspecified for multichannel and multi-head correlations
comments.quote: where arg Topk( _·_ ) is to get the arguments of the Topk autocorrelations and let _k_ = _⌊c ×_ log _L⌋_ , _c_ is a hyper-parameter. _RQ,K_ is autocorrelation between series _Q_ and _K_ .
comments.explanation: The high-level idea is clear: compute lag correlations, select the largest \(k\) delays, and aggregate rolled values using softmax-normalized correlation scores. What remains unclear is the dimensional reduction used to obtain those top-k delays when \(Q\) and \(K\) are multivariate tensors rather than scalar series. Earlier and later notation indicates hidden channels and multiple heads, so \(R_{Q,K}(\tau)\) could be a scalar per lag, a vector per channel, or even head-/batch-specific. These alternatives imply different algorithms: one global delay set shared across channels, separate delay sets per head, separate delay sets per sample, or per-channel delay sets. The surrounding context establishes the intended mechanism but does not fully specify this reduction step, so reproducibility would require an implementation detail not provided in the prose shown here.

comments.title: The method calls \(R_{Q,K}\) an autocorrelation even when it is mathematically a cross-correlation
comments.quote: _RQ,K_ is autocorrelation between series _Q_ and _K_ .
comments.explanation: I can see why the authors use the term Auto-Correlation: in self-style usage \(Q\), \(K\), and \(V\) are projections derived from the same hidden sequence, and the mechanism is meant to replace self-attention by a lag-correlation operator. Still, once the projected query and key are distinct series, \(R_{Q,K}\) is conventionally a cross-correlation, not an autocorrelation. The distinction becomes more than terminology in the encoder-decoder case, where the passage says \(Q\) comes from the decoder while \(K,V\) come from the encoder. The computational recipe via Fourier products remains valid using the cross-correlation analogue of Wiener--Khinchin, so the method is likely fixable as stated. But the terminology could mislead readers about the exact statistic being computed.

comments.title: Final decoder output does not explicitly state that only the forecast horizon is used
comments.quote: The final prediction is the sum of the two refined decomposed components, as _WS ∗X_ de _[M]_[+] _[ T]_ de _[M]_[,] where _WS_ is to project the deep transformed seasonal component _X_ de _[M]_[to the target dimension.]
comments.explanation: The decomposition and accumulation story is coherent, and the expression correctly conveys that the final output combines seasonal and trend-cyclical components. However, the decoder input was earlier defined to have length \(I/2+O\), because it includes a known recent-history segment plus \(O\) future placeholders. Therefore the summed decoder output should also naturally have length \(I/2+O\), while the forecasting target has length \(O\). The prose calls the whole sum 'the final prediction' without saying whether the model discards the initial \(I/2\) positions and evaluates only the last \(O\) time steps. Most forecasting readers may infer this from the placeholder construction, but the formal description would be clearer and less ambiguous if it explicitly specified the final slicing.

comments.title: Some Traffic MAE entries are bolded even though they are not the lowest
comments.explanation: I first interpreted the boldface as indicating the best result, which is the convention stated for the tables: lower MSE or MAE is better. Checking the Traffic row against the listed baselines, Autoformer has the best MSE for all four horizons, but its MAE is not always the best. At prediction length 96, Autoformer’s MAE is 0.388, while LogTrans is lower at 0.384. At prediction length 720, Autoformer’s MAE is 0.408, while LogTrans is lower at 0.396. But because the table visually marks Autoformer as best on MAE in cases where the listed values contradict that, it can mislead readers about per-metric state-of-the-art performance.

comments.title: Prediction-length description does not match the ILI row
comments.explanation: The surrounding caption describes the multivariate table as using prediction lengths 96, 192, 336, and 720, and also notes that the ILI input length is different. Looking at the actual ILI row, however, the prediction lengths are 24, 36, 48, and 60. This is understandable because ILI is weekly and much shorter-frequency than the other datasets, so using smaller horizons is probably intentional rather than a numerical mistake. Still, the text does not explicitly say that ILI is an exception to the stated set of prediction lengths. A careful reader could therefore initially assume that all datasets share the same horizons and then be confused by the ILI row. The fix would be a small qualification in the caption, e.g. that ILI uses horizons 24/36/48/60 while the other multivariate datasets use 96/192/336/720.

comments.title: Auto-Correlation hyperparameter selection is underspecified
comments.quote: The hyper-parameter _c_ of Auto-Correlation is in the range of 1 to 3 to trade off performance and efficiency.
comments.explanation: The paper correctly identifies that the Auto-Correlation mechanism depends on the hyperparameter c through k = floor(c log L), so c directly changes both the number of selected delays and the computational/accuracy tradeoff. I checked whether the nearby implementation details specify a fixed value, a validation-selection rule, or dataset-specific settings; the passage only gives a range and points to supplementary sensitivity analysis, not the actual values used to produce the reported tables. Sensitivity analysis is useful, but it does not by itself tell a reader how to reproduce the main results. Because c is part of the core proposed mechanism and can affect both performance and efficiency, the experimental protocol should state whether c was fixed globally, tuned per dataset/horizon, or chosen by validation, and what values were used.

comments.title: Univariate MSE reduction percentage does not match the stated numbers
comments.quote: for the input-96-predict-336 setting, our model achieves **14%** (0.180 _→_ 0.145) MSE reduction on the ETT dataset
comments.explanation: The qualitative point is clear: Autoformer improves over the previous best univariate ETT result in this setting. However, checking the arithmetic from the numbers printed in the text gives \((0.180-0.145)/0.180 \approx 0.194\), i.e. about a 19% reduction, not 14%. I considered whether this could be due to rounding or whether 0.180 might not be the actual comparator used for the percentage, but the parenthetical explicitly presents 0.180 \(\to\) 0.145 as the basis for the reduction. A 14% reduction would correspond to a previous value around 0.169 rather than 0.180. This is localized, but it is a real numerical inconsistency in a reported headline improvement.

comments.title: Some reported percentage reductions are not reproducible from the displayed values
comments.quote: Autoformer gives **74%** (1.334 _→_ 0.339) MSE reduction in ETT, **18%** (0.280 _→_ 0.231) in Electricity, **61%** (1.357 _→_ 0.509) in Exchange, **15%** (0.733 _→_ 0.622) in Traffic and **21%** (0.455 _→_ 0.359) in Weather. For the input36-predict-60 setting of ILI, Autoformer makes **43%** (4.882 _→_ 2.770) MSE reduction. Overall, Autoformer yields a **38%** averaged MSE reduction among above settings.
comments.explanation: Most of these figures are close to what one obtains from the displayed before-and-after MSE values, so the intended claim is understandable. But at least the Exchange percentage is not consistent with the printed numbers: \((1.357-0.509)/1.357 \approx 62.5\%\), not 61%. The overall 38% figure is also hard to reproduce from the immediately listed examples: averaging the displayed relative reductions gives roughly 39%, while computing a pooled reduction from the displayed MSEs gives roughly 47%. I checked whether context resolves this by interpreting the 38% as an average over all Table 1 settings rather than just the six examples, which may indeed be the authors' intended meaning. If so, the wording 'among above settings' is ambiguous, because it follows a specific list of six reductions. The issue is therefore not the direction of the result, but the reproducibility and clarity of the reported summary statistic.

comments.title: Baseline citations appear swapped in the ablation table header
comments.quote: |Input-96|Transformer[35]|Informer[41]|LogTrans[17]|Reformer[20]|Promotion|
comments.explanation: Earlier in the paper and in the surrounding context, LogTrans is consistently associated with reference [20], while Reformer is associated with reference [17]. The numerical columns themselves appear to correspond to the intended LogTrans and Reformer baselines, so this does not seem to affect the experimental results. I also considered whether this could be an OCR artifact or table-extraction error; nevertheless, if the passage is taken literally, it misattributes the two baselines in this table. This is a small but real consistency issue for readers tracing the compared methods.

comments.title: Ambiguity in how learned lags are aggregated for Figure 6
comments.quote: Figure 6: Statistics of learned lags. For each time series in the test set, we count the top 10 lags learned by decoder for the input-96-predict-336 task. Figure (a)-(d) are the density histograms.
comments.explanation: I understand the intent: the authors want to make the Auto-Correlation mechanism interpretable by showing that the delays selected by the decoder concentrate around meaningful calendar periods. That is a useful diagnostic, and it is consistent with the mechanism introduced earlier, where top-k delays \(\tau_i\) are selected from autocorrelation scores. However, after checking the architecture, there are several unresolved choices hidden in the phrase “top 10 lags learned by decoder.” The decoder can include multiple Auto-Correlation blocks, multiple heads, and possibly multiple layers; the lags may be selected per sample, per head, per channel, or after averaging autocorrelation scores. It is also unclear whether the counted lags come from decoder self Auto-Correlation, encoder-decoder Auto-Correlation, or both. Since the histogram is used to support an interpretability claim, these aggregation choices matter: different heads or blocks could learn different delays, and averaging could create or obscure peaks. This does not undermine the forecasting method itself, but the diagnostic is underspecified enough that a reader could not reproduce the figure or know exactly what “learned lags” refers to.

comments.title: Exchange-period interpretation is stronger than the evidence shown
comments.quote: daily recorded Exchange dataset present the monthly, quarterly and yearly periods (Figure 6 (b)).
comments.explanation: The authors are plausibly interpreting peaks in the learned-lag histogram: for daily data, lags near roughly 30, 90, and 365 days naturally invite monthly, quarterly, and yearly labels. I also recognize that the claim is part of a qualitative analysis rather than the main quantitative result. Still, in the broader context the Exchange dataset is described as lacking obvious periodicity, and exchange-rate series are often noisy and only weakly seasonal. A histogram of model-selected lags suggests that the model uses delays near those calendar scales, but it does not by itself establish that the dataset truly “presents” monthly, quarterly, and yearly periods. Those peaks could reflect calendar conventions, windowing effects, the top-k selection procedure, or model bias rather than validated seasonal structure. A more cautious wording such as “the learned lags concentrate near monthly, quarterly, and yearly scales” would be better supported.

comments.title: Empirical efficiency setup lacks key reproducibility details
comments.quote: Figure 7: Efficiency Analysis. For memory, we replace Auto-Correlation with self-attention family in Autoformer and record the memory with input 96. For running time, we run the Auto-Correlation or self-attentions 10[3] times to get the execution time per step. The output length increases exponentially.
comments.explanation: The caption helpfully distinguishes the two measurements: memory is measured by replacing Auto-Correlation inside Autoformer, while runtime is measured by repeatedly running the attention/correlation modules. That resolves a possible confusion about whether both panels use exactly the same benchmark. What remains underspecified, however, are details that materially affect memory and timing comparisons: batch size, model dimension, number of heads, number of layers or whether gradients are retained, precision, exact sequence length passed to each mechanism, CUDA/PyTorch FFT implementation, and whether peak training memory includes activations, optimizer state, or only module tensors. These constants matter especially when comparing FFT-based Auto-Correlation with LSH, ProbSparse, and full attention, whose practical performance can depend heavily on implementation. The theoretical \(O(L\log L)\) discussion supports the qualitative claim, but the empirical Figure 7 is not fully reproducible from the information given.

comments.title: Interpretability claim overstates what the lag visualizations establish
comments.quote: These results show that Autoformer can capture the complex seasonalities of real-world series from deep representations and further provide a human-interpretable prediction.
comments.explanation: The visualizations do provide a reasonable and interesting form of interpretability: the selected delays can be inspected and often align with known daily, weekly, monthly, or yearly scales. That is a strength of the mechanism compared with opaque point-wise attention weights. However, the final phrase “provide a human-interpretable prediction” is stronger than what has been demonstrated in this passage. The figures interpret selected lag statistics, not the full prediction, and they do not show how much each lag contributed to a particular forecast value, how seasonal and trend components combine for an individual example, or whether the identified lags are necessary for the forecast. A careful reader could interpret this as claiming instance-level forecast explanations, while the evidence shown supports the narrower claim that Autoformer’s learned dependencies are partly interpretable.

progressive__claude-opus-4.7.label: Progressive (claude-opus-4.7)
progressive__claude-opus-4.7.overall_feedback: This paper presents Autoformer, a Transformer-based architecture for long-term time series forecasting that integrates series decomposition as an inner block and replaces self-attention with an Auto-Correlation mechanism operating at the sub-series level. Overall, the paper is well-motivated and clearly written: the authors articulate two concrete limitations of prior Transformer-based forecasters (entangled temporal patterns and the point-wise sparsity/information-utilization tradeoff) and tie each proposed component directly to one of these limitations, which makes the contribution narrative easy to follow. The claimed contributions are substantive—moving decomposition from pre-processing into a progressive inner operator is a conceptually appealing design, and grounding the Auto-Correlation mechanism in stochastic process theory with O(L log L) complexity is an attractive efficiency/accuracy proposition—backed by a headline 38% relative improvement across six benchmarks spanning five domains. That said, several concerns warrant scrutiny in the remainder of the paper: (1) the 38% figure is aggregated and the abstract/introduction do not yet clarify how it is computed or against which baselines, raising questions about cherry-picking across horizons and metrics; (2) the novelty of Auto-Correlation relative to existing frequency-domain and periodicity-based attention variants (e.g., FFT-based or seasonal attention methods) needs sharper differentiation; (3) the mechanism implicitly assumes well-defined periodicity, and it is unclear how it behaves on aperiodic or multi-scale-periodic series, which is common in economics and disease data; and (4) the claim that point-wise sparse attention is the key bottleneck is asserted rather than demonstrated empirically in the introduction. Clearer ablations isolating the contributions of the decomposition block versus Auto-Correlation, and a more careful positioning against concurrent efficient-Transformer forecasters (Informer, LogTrans, Reformer), will be important for assessing the paper’s true advance.

comments.title: Encoder-decoder Auto-Correlation with resized K,V breaks the autocorrelation interpretation
comments.quote: For the encoder-decoder Auto-Correlation (Figure 1), _K, V_ are from the encoder _X_ en _[N]_[and will be resized to length-] _[O]_[,] _[ Q]_[ is from the previous block of the decoder.]
comments.explanation: The Auto-Correlation mechanism is defined in terms of the autocorrelation R_{Q,K}(τ), which requires Q and K to be of the same length so that the Wiener–Khinchin / FFT-based computation makes sense. Here, the encoder output has length I, but the decoder input has length I/2 + O, so Q and K are of different lengths. The text says K, V will be 'resized to length-O,' but Q from the decoder has length I/2 + O, not O. This creates an inconsistency: either Q and K are not the same length (in which case the FFT-based autocorrelation computation in Eq. 5/8 is underspecified), or the resizing target is wrong. The paper does not specify what 'resize' means (interpolation? padding? truncation?), which is a reproducibility gap for a key component.

comments.title: Final prediction formula notation for W_S application is ambiguous
comments.quote: The final prediction is the sum of the two refined decomposed components, as _WS ∗X_ de _[M]_[+] _[ T]_ de _[M]_[,]
comments.explanation: X_de^M has shape (I/2 + O) × d_model after the decoder, but the final prediction should have length O. The formula W_S * X_de^M + T_de^M suggests a linear projection on the channel dimension only (d_model → target dimension), but does not address how the length dimension is reduced from I/2 + O to O. A careful reader would wonder whether the last O positions are sliced out, or whether the decoder initialization is such that only the last O positions are used. This is a minor gap in the description of the output head but is resolvable by inspecting the code.

comments.title: Inconsistency in Traffic MAE at O=336
comments.explanation: Looking at the Traffic results row for O=336, Autoformer reports MSE=0.622 and MAE=0.337. This MAE value (0.337) is notably lower than the MAE values at O=96 (0.388), O=192 (0.382), and O=720 (0.408) for the same model. Since MSE increases monotonically with O (0.613, 0.616, 0.622, 0.660), one would normally expect MAE to follow a similar trend. An MAE of 0.337 at O=336 sandwiched between 0.382 and 0.408 is suspicious and likely an OCR or transcription error (possibly should be 0.387 or similar). This could mislead readers about the model's performance scaling.

comments.title: LogTrans complexity stated as O(L(log L)^2) compared apples-to-oranges with per-layer complexities
comments.quote: LogTrans [20] introduces the local convolution to Transformer and proposes the LogSparse attention to select time steps following the exponentially increasing intervals, which reduces the complexity to _O_ ( _L_ (log _L_ )[2] ).
comments.explanation: The LogSparse attention in Li et al. (NeurIPS 2019) is O(L(log L)^2) memory when stacking log L layers; per-layer it's O(L log L). The comparison with Reformer at O(L log L) and Informer at O(L log L) is then an apples-to-oranges comparison if those are per-layer complexities while LogTrans is cumulative. A careful reader could be misled into thinking LogTrans is strictly worse asymptotically when the difference may be due to how the complexities are counted.

comments.title: Efficiency analysis setup does not clarify what memory is measured
comments.quote: For memory, we replace Auto-Correlation with self-attention family in Autoformer and record the memory with input 96.
comments.explanation: The figure's x-axis shows output lengths up to very large values, but the input is fixed at 96. In Autoformer's decoder, the self-attention/Auto-Correlation operates on sequences of length I/2 + O, while the encoder's sequence length stays at 96 regardless. The text doesn't explicitly state whether the plotted 'memory' reflects full model memory or just the attention module, which could mislead readers trying to reproduce the figure.

progressive_original__claude-opus-4.7.label: Progressive Original (claude-opus-4.7)
progressive_original__claude-opus-4.7.overall_feedback: This paper presents Autoformer, a Transformer-based architecture for long-term time series forecasting that integrates series decomposition as an inner block and replaces self-attention with an Auto-Correlation mechanism operating at the sub-series level. Overall, the paper is well-motivated and clearly written: the authors articulate two concrete limitations of prior Transformer-based forecasters (entangled temporal patterns and the point-wise sparsity/information-utilization tradeoff) and tie each proposed component directly to one of these limitations, which makes the contribution narrative easy to follow. The claimed contributions are substantive—moving decomposition from pre-processing into a progressive inner operator is a conceptually appealing design, and grounding the Auto-Correlation mechanism in stochastic process theory with O(L log L) complexity is an attractive efficiency/accuracy proposition—backed by a headline 38% relative improvement across six benchmarks spanning five domains. That said, several concerns warrant scrutiny in the remainder of the paper: (1) the 38% figure is aggregated and the abstract/introduction do not yet clarify how it is computed or against which baselines, raising questions about cherry-picking across horizons and metrics; (2) the novelty of Auto-Correlation relative to existing frequency-domain and periodicity-based attention variants (e.g., FFT-based or seasonal attention methods) needs sharper differentiation; (3) the mechanism implicitly assumes well-defined periodicity, and it is unclear how it behaves on aperiodic or multi-scale-periodic series, which is common in economics and disease data; and (4) the claim that point-wise sparse attention is the key bottleneck is asserted rather than demonstrated empirically in the introduction. Clearer ablations isolating the contributions of the decomposition block versus Auto-Correlation, and a more careful positioning against concurrent efficient-Transformer forecasters (Informer, LogTrans, Reformer), will be important for assessing the paper’s true advance.

comments.title: LogTrans complexity stated as O(L(log L)^2) contradicts standard result
comments.quote: LogTrans [20] introduces the local convolution to Transformer and proposes the LogSparse attention to select time steps following the exponentially increasing intervals, which reduces the complexity to _O_ ( _L_ (log _L_ )[2] ).
comments.explanation: Initially I wondered whether this is a genuine error or the original paper's stated complexity. The LogSparse attention in Li et al. (NeurIPS 2019) is typically reported as O(L(log L)^2) memory when stacking log L layers; per-layer it's O(L log L). This is actually consistent with what Li et al. report, so this may not be an error — it's the overall complexity when the LogSparse scheme is applied with stacking. However, the comparison with Reformer at O(L log L) and Informer at O(L log L) is then an apples-to-oranges comparison if those are per-layer complexities while LogTrans is cumulative. A careful reader could be misled into thinking LogTrans is strictly worse asymptotically when the difference may be due to how the complexities are counted. Since the authors are summarizing the prior papers' own stated complexities, this is at most a mild concern.

comments.title: Reformer attention name: 'local-sensitive hashing' should be 'locality-sensitive hashing'
comments.quote: Reformer [17] presents the local-sensitive hashing (LSH) attention
comments.explanation: The standard name is 'locality-sensitive hashing'. This is a terminology slip that readers will easily recognize, but it is a factual mislabeling of a named technique. Given the instruction not to flag typos or formatting, and that 'LSH' is correctly expanded to the right thing in substance (the abbreviation L-S-H matches locality-sensitive), this is borderline — the abbreviation is right but the expansion is wrong. I'll note it as minor since the correct method is clearly identified by the abbreviation and citation.

comments.title: Informer's ProbSparse attention described as 'KL-divergence based'
comments.quote: Informer [41] extends Transformer with KL-divergence based ProbSparse
comments.explanation: This is consistent with Informer's own motivation: they derive a query sparsity measurement from a KL-divergence between the attention distribution and a uniform distribution. So this description is accurate. No issue.

comments.title: Encoder-decoder Auto-Correlation with resized K,V breaks the autocorrelation interpretation
comments.quote: For the encoder-decoder Auto-Correlation (Figure 1), _K, V_ are from the encoder _X_ en _[N]_[and will be resized to length-] _[O]_[,] _[ Q]_[ is from the previous block of the decoder.]

Here, the encoder output has length I, but the decoder input has length I/2 + O, so Q and K are of different lengths. The text says K, V will be 'resized to length-O,' but Q from the decoder has length I/2 + O, not O. This creates an inconsistency: either Q and K are not the same length (in which case the FFT-based autocorrelation computation in Eq. 5/8 is underspecified), or the resizing target is wrong. The paper does not specify what 'resize' means (interpolation? padding? truncation?), which is a reproducibility gap for a key component.

comments.title: Final prediction formula notation for W_S application is ambiguous
comments.quote: The final prediction is the sum of the two refined decomposed components, as _WS ∗X_ de _[M]_[+] _[ T]_ de _[M]_[,]
comments.explanation: X_de^M has shape (I/2 + O) × d_model after the decoder, but the final prediction should have length O. The formula W_S * X_de^M + T_de^M suggests a linear projection on the channel dimension only (d_model → target dimension), but does not address how the length dimension is reduced from I/2 + O to O. A careful reader would wonder whether the last O positions are sliced out, or whether the decoder initialization is such that only the last O positions are used. This is a minor gap in the description of the output head but is resolvable by inspecting the code.

comments.title: Inconsistency in Traffic MAE at O=336
comments.explanation: Looking at the Traffic results row for O=336, Autoformer reports MSE=0.622 and MAE=0.337. This MAE value (0.337) is notably lower than the MAE values at O=96 (0.388), O=192 (0.382), and O=720 (0.408) for the same model. Since MSE increases monotonically with O (0.613, 0.616, 0.622, 0.660), one would normally expect MAE to follow a similar trend. An MAE of 0.337 at O=336 sandwiched between 0.382 and 0.408 is suspicious and likely an OCR or transcription error (possibly should be 0.387 or similar). This could mislead readers about the model's performance scaling. Worth flagging because if it's a genuine reported number, it's an outlier that deserves comment; if it's an error, it misrepresents the result.

comments.title: Univariate Exchange 96 bolding/winner inconsistency
comments.explanation: In Table 2 for Exchange at O=96, the best result appears to be ARIMA at 0.112/0.245 (bolded), while Autoformer's 0.241/0.387 is not bolded. This is consistent — ARIMA wins here. However, the text claims overall SOTA and a 38% relative improvement; this particular cell is a case where Autoformer is beaten. This is not an error in the table, just noting that the paper's general framing of SOTA dominance has at least one exception visible in the shown data. Not flagging as a real issue since Table 2 correctly identifies the winner and the paper's claim is an aggregate improvement, not universal dominance. Including only as a sanity check — no action needed.

comments.title: Complexity claim for aggregation step
comments.quote: Because we aggregate _O_ (log _L_ ) series whose length is _L_ , the complexity of Equations 6 and 7 is _O_ ( _L_ log _L_ ).
comments.explanation: I initially wanted to verify the complexity argument. The mechanism aggregates k = ⌊c × log L⌋ rolled series, each of length L. Each roll and weighted sum is O(L), so the aggregation is O(k·L) = O(L log L). The FFT-based autocorrelation computation is O(L log L). So total is O(L log L). This checks out. The claim is correctly justified — no issue.

comments.title: Implausible 74% MSE reduction claim for ETT input-96-predict-336
comments.quote: Autoformer gives **74%** (1.334 _→_ 0.339) MSE reduction in ETT
comments.explanation: Initially I tried to reconcile this with Table 1. The passage states the baseline MSE to beat is 1.334, and Autoformer achieves 0.339. Checking the ablation Table 3, 1.334 appears as the LogTrans Origin result at predict-336, and the Autoformer-matching value 0.339 also appears in the main-table excerpt. However, the claim 'compared to previous state-of-the-art results' means the baseline should be the best (lowest MSE) of the prior methods, not just one of them. Looking at Table 3's predict-336 row: Transformer Origin=1.413, Informer=1.363, LogTrans=1.334, Reformer=1.549 — so 1.334 is indeed the best among these, and (1.334−0.339)/1.334 ≈ 74.6%. That checks out arithmetically. The concern is narrower: the reduction is computed against only Transformer-family baselines within the ablation table, while the main Table 1 includes LSTNet, LSTM, TCN baselines that might be stronger. Without the full Table 1 values visible, I can't fully verify, but the 74% figure appears to rely on a specific baseline choice; still, given the setup this is likely correct. Marking as minor because the arithmetic is consistent with at least one reasonable interpretation.

comments.title: Overall 38% average computation appears to mix heterogeneous settings
comments.quote: Overall, Autoformer yields a **38%** averaged MSE reduction among above settings.
comments.explanation: The authors list five percentage improvements for predict-336 (74%, 18%, 61%, 15%, 21%) plus one for ILI predict-60 (43%). Averaging these six: (74+18+61+15+21+43)/6 ≈ 38.7%, which matches the 38% claim. So the arithmetic works. However, the abstract and introduction state '38% relative improvement on six benchmarks' more broadly, while here it is computed from only one prediction length per dataset (plus ILI's specific horizon). A careful reader might wonder whether the 38% holds when averaged across all four prediction lengths. Since the derivation here is internally consistent, this is minor framing rather than an error.

comments.title: Efficiency analysis uses input length 96 but varies output length to very large values without clarifying attention sequence length
comments.quote: For memory, we replace Auto-Correlation with self-attention family in Autoformer and record the memory with input 96.
comments.explanation: Initially I wanted to verify that the O(L log L) complexity claim is supported by the figure. The figure's x-axis shows output lengths up to 8192 (and memory up to 3072/5121), but the input is fixed at 96. In Autoformer's decoder, the self-attention/Auto-Correlation operates on sequences of length I/2 + O, and encoder-decoder attention involves K,V resized to length O. So the relevant L for the decoder scales with O, which is fine. However, the encoder's sequence length stays at 96 regardless, so encoder attention contributes a constant. The efficiency comparison is therefore dominated by the decoder. This is a reasonable setup, but the text doesn't explicitly state this, and a reader might wonder whether the plotted 'memory' reflects full model memory or just the attention module. The ambiguity is mild but could mislead readers trying to reproduce the figure.
